It was October 2nd.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> And it was close to my home.<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We lived at the time in Lancaster County, Pennsylvania.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> I had just put our children on the school bus<|9.00|> <|9.00|> and was out running errands when the news broke.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> There was an incident just a few miles away<|20.00|> <|20.00|> at a one-room Amish schoolhouse.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There were young girls being held captive and being shot.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> It was beyond horrifying.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> And as the news continued to unfold,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> and the number of the deceased grew,<|21.00|> <|21.00|> six in all, five young girls and the perpetrator,<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The entire county and then country was in shock.<|6.72|> <|8.28|> The media descended on the normally peaceful and bucolic farmland.<|13.72|> <|15.10|> Helicopters buzzed overhead.<|17.48|> <|18.42|> TV trucks were parked up and down rural roadways, many of them dirt roads.<|25.64|> <|25.64|> The city was in shock.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and reporters jockeyed for position near the schoolhouse.<|5.50|> <|5.50|> And while everyone was still reeling from the tragedy,<|12.50|> <|12.50|> the focus of the media attention started to shift.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was no longer simply the story of another and far too often occurring school shooting.<|10.38|> <|11.52|> Suddenly the story became about the Amish community's response to the shooter's family.<|20.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank we must not think evil of this man.<|14.82|> <|15.84|> He had a mother and a wife and a soul, and he is now standing before a just God.<|26.02|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.38|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There were members of the Amish community who went to the perpetrator's family that very day.<|9.66|> <|10.66|> One Amish man held the shooter's father in his arms while that father wept over what his son had done and over his son's death.<|23.68|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They tended to the widow and her children, and more than 30 members of that community,<|9.34|> <|9.34|> from the Nickel Mines community, attended the shooter's funeral to comfort and support the widow.<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Forgiveness. Wild, abundant, extravagant, and certainly countercultural forgiveness.<|14.50|> <|14.88|> The very forgiveness that Joseph showed his brothers who so deeply wronged him.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The kind of forgiveness that comes when we suspend judgment as Paul exhorts readers in his letter to the Romans.<|10.28|> <|10.64|> The kind of extravagant forgiveness Jesus demands in his telling Peter that he must forgive not just seven times,<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but 77 times, whereas the King James Version tells us 70 times 7.<|10.22|> <|12.34|> The merciful forgiveness the king shows the first slave in Jesus' parable,<|19.24|> <|20.24|> the kind of forgiveness that makes no sense to the world<|25.18|> <|25.18|> and is therefore newsworthy.<|28.06|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> it makes no sense except that we have been forgiven.<|10.24|> <|12.36|> You see, the Amish live out these texts.<|17.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.08|> They not only read them, but they inwardly digest them and live them.<|7.24|> <|8.72|> Forgiveness is foundational in their life together in community.<|12.68|> <|12.68|> It is the bedrock of their faith and how they relate to one another on a daily basis.<|20.56|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.26|> Forgiveness is embedded in their society through five centuries of Anabaptist tradition.<|8.08|> <|8.40|> And it is grounded in the firm belief that a life in Christ demands forgiveness.<|17.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The community's acts of forgiveness were not isolated decisions by a few saintly individuals,<|10.72|> <|10.72|> but rather happened because of intentional practices that are supported by all aspects of their life.<|20.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Amish response reflected the sect's heritage and deeply embedded faith.<|9.86|> <|9.86|> They understand that forgiveness and pardon are not the same thing.<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As the community elder said, we must not think evil of this man.<|7.24|> <|8.24|> He had a mother and a wife and a soul, and he is now standing before a just God.<|16.92|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Forgiveness relinquishes the right to vengeance, while pardon forfeits punishment altogether.<|13.50|> <|14.50|> They are not the same thing.<|16.28|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Forgiveness leads to reconciliation, and reconciliation restores relationship within a community of a victim and an offender, where it creates a whole new relationship.<|15.80|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Amish willingness to forego vengeance does not undo the tragedy or pardon the wrong,<|9.54|> <|10.38|> but rather it constitutes a first step toward a future that is more hopeful.<|17.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and certainly more compassionate.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> And indeed, the Nickel Mines Schoolhouse was raised,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> and a new one-room schoolhouse was built in a nearby location,<|19.00|> <|19.00|> and they named it the New Hope School.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The world was first shocked by the tragedy, but then further shocked by the show of compassion.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> My friends, as followers of Christ, should we really be shocked?<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If we clearly comprehend the forgiveness we have been extended by God,<|7.60|> <|8.24|> shouldn't we desire to share that same abundant forgiveness as well?<|15.50|> <|16.70|> Do we take to heart the words we say each week,<|20.94|> <|21.68|> forgive our trespasses as we forgive those who trespass against us?<|27.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When we practice forgiveness on the little things, the daily offenses that can take root in our heart and grow into deep bitterness,<|15.36|> <|16.36|> when we are in the habit of forgiving daily,<|21.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we can more easily understand forgiving things that seem unforgivable.<|7.78|> <|9.32|> Wild, abundant, extravagant forgiveness.<|14.18|> <|15.34|> Just one of the many gifts we are given by God,<|18.96|> <|18.96|> and out of that gift, we are called to give.<|24.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.12|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus' parable tells of the wicked behavior of the forgiven slave who refuses to forgive those who owe him a debt.<|12.08|> <|13.38|> He has received and refuses to extend forgiveness.<|18.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Last week, we heard the lectionary, the verses just prior to this, are Jesus' instruction on how to handle conflict within a faith community.<|15.08|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It will be familiar to many of you.<|3.76|> <|3.76|> First, go to the person who has sinned against you.<|8.84|> <|8.84|> Tell them what is wrong.<|11.94|> <|11.94|> And if they do not repent, bring two or three others into the conversation.<|18.66|> <|18.66|> And if the offender still will not repent, bring the disagreement before the entire community.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this is not about ganging up on someone.<|4.58|> <|4.58|> This is first off about being direct, a thing that is so hard for many of us.<|12.04|> <|12.04|> Being direct with the person with whom you have conflict and not triangulating, not gossiping,<|19.28|> <|19.28|> not talking behind anyone's back.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus is explaining how to live in community,<|6.74|> <|6.74|> because, my friends, living in community is hard.<|10.12|> <|11.62|> How to treat each other well,<|14.26|> <|15.38|> and that even in conflict, he says,<|18.22|> <|18.82|> for where two or three are gathered in my name,<|22.64|> <|23.14|> I am there among them.<|25.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is immediately following this exchange that Peter asks how many times he has to forgive.<|9.34|> <|11.08|> Typical Peter, right?<|12.76|> <|13.96|> Okay, Jesus.<|14.68|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This all sounds well and good.<|2.58|> <|2.70|> I'll do that direct communication thing.<|5.28|> <|5.80|> I'll listen.<|6.52|>
